title: Integrated multi-omics analysis reveals an interplay of hormonal regulation, antioxidant defense, and lipid signaling during artificial seed aging in Momordica charantia.
author_string: Yingchutrakul, Yodying, Kumsab, Jakkaphan, Simanon, Nattapon, Tangphatsornruang, Sithichoke, Butkinaree, Chutikarn
abstract: Momordica charantia L. (bitter melon) is a nutritionally and medicinally important crop whose seed viability declines rapidly under heat and humidity. This study aimed to elucidate the molecular mechanisms of artificial seed aging using an integrated proteomics, metabolomics, and hormone analysis approach. Germination rate decreased by nearly 50 % after 18 days of high-temperature and moisture stress. Proteomic profiling identified 42 differentially expressed proteins, including a transient increase in superoxide dismutase (SOD) at day 9 post-stress followed by a marked decline in peroxidase (POD) and peroxiredoxin at day 18, indicating progressive oxidative damage. Metabolomics annotated 334 metabolites, revealing significant remodeling in the lipoxygenase pathway, characterized by elevated nonadienal, linoleate, and α-linoleate associated with lipid peroxidation. Quantitative hormone analysis showed a 1.1-fold rise in gibberellic acid (GA<sub>3</sub>) at day 9 post-stress and a 1.3-fold increase in abscisic acid (ABA) at day 18 post-stress, reflecting a switch from germination promotion to dormancy induction. Together, these data demonstrate that the combined failure of antioxidant defenses and the shift toward ABA dominance accelerate seed deterioration. This work establishes biochemical markers linking oxidative stress, lipid signaling, and hormonal imbalance, providing molecular insight to improve seed storage and viability management in tropical crops.
